g.data-narration: CPU scheduling determines which process runs on the CPU at any time in a multiprogramming system where multiple processes share the processor.
g.text: A fundamental OS function that selects which process executes on the CPU at any given time.
In multiprogramming, multiple processes reside in memory. The scheduler selects
from the ready queue when CPU becomes idle. This is pure overhead work.

g.data-narration: The main goals of CPU scheduling are maximizing CPU utilization and throughput while minimizing turnaround, waiting, and response times.
We evaluate scheduling algorithms using five key metrics: CPU utilization, throughput, turnaround time, waiting time, and response time.
Turnaround time is completion minus arrival. Waiting time equals turnaround minus burst time. Response time is first response minus arrival.
Processes move between job queue, ready queue, and device queues during their lifecycle. The OS manages this transition for efficient execution.
Three scheduler types work together: long-term controls degree of multiprogramming, short-term selects next process, and medium-term swaps processes.
Context switch saves process state to PCB, then loads new process state. This pure overhead consumes CPU time without doing useful work.
Preemptive scheduling allows process interruption mid-execution. Non-preemptive holds CPU until process voluntarily yields or terminates.
g.text: CPU kept until process waits/terminates • FCFS, Non-preemptive SJF
Process can be interrupted • RR, Preemptive SJF (SRTF)

g.data-narration: FCFS serves processes in arrival order. Simple but suffers convoy effect where short processes wait behind long ones causing high waiting time.
g.text: Example: P1(24), P2(3), P3(3) - all arrive at time 0
Waiting: P1=0, P2=24, P3=27 • Avg Waiting = (0+24+27)/3 = 17

g.data-narration: SJF selects shortest next CPU burst. It's provably optimal for minimum average waiting time but requires predicting unknown burst lengths.
g.text: Example: P1(6), P2(8), P3(7), P4(3) - all arrive at time 0
Optimal for average waiting time! (vs FCFS: 17 → 7)

g.data-narration: Priority scheduling runs highest priority process first. Lower priority processes may starve. Aging gradually increases priority of waiting processes to prevent starvation.
g.text: Example: P1(10, priority=3), P2(1, priority=1), P3(2, priority=4), P4(1, priority=2)
Problem: Starvation - low priority processes wait indefinitely. Solution: Aging - gradually increase priority of waiting processes.

g.data-narration: Round Robin gives each process a time quantum. Processes are preempted after quantum expires and moved to ready queue end. Fair for time-sharing systems.
g.text: Example: P1(24), P2(3), P3(3) • Time Quantum = 4
Waiting: P1=6, P2=4, P3=7 • Avg Waiting = 5.67 (better than FCFS=17!)
Note: If quantum → ∞, RR = FCFS. If quantum → 0, high context switch overhead.

g.data-narration: FCFS is simple but slow, SJF is optimal but hard to implement, Priority risks starvation, RR is fair but has overhead. No algorithm is best for all scenarios.
Key takeaways: Draw Gantt charts first, calculate waiting time as turnaround minus burst, and remember preemptive vs non-preemptive variants produce different results.

g.text: Always draw Gantt charts first - makes calculations easier
Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time
SJF is optimal but impractical (can't predict burst)
RR: quantum too small = high overhead

g.data-narration: CPU scheduling is foundational to OS design. Understanding these algorithms prepares you to analyze real-world system performance and optimization.
g.text: 🎯 Master CPU Scheduling: Foundation for System Performance Analysis
